topics.label: Introduction: Origins of Technology & Life Cycle Stages
objectives: Explain how technologies emerge from scientific, social, and economic conditions
Describe the key stages of a technology life cycle
Apply life cycle models (e.g., Gartner Hype Cycle, S-Curve) to a current technology
Identify methods for tracking and assessing emerging technologies
common_confusions: Confusing invention (first creation) with emergence (societal uptake)
Treating life cycle stages as fixed timelines rather than variable trajectories
Conflating the Gartner Hype Cycle with actual market adoption data
example_applications: Mapping generative AI onto the Gartner Hype Cycle
Tracing the S-curve of smartphone adoption from 2007–2015
Using Forrester's Technology Radar to track enterprise software trends

topics.label: Creative Destruction: Economic Forces Behind Emerging Technologies
objectives: Define creative destruction and its role in economic evolution
Identify economic incentives that drive technology creation and displacement
Analyze industry examples where creative destruction reshaped markets
Evaluate the winners and losers of a creative destruction event
common_confusions: Assuming creative destruction is always negative — it simultaneously destroys and creates value
Conflating disruption (market restructuring) with incremental improvement
Underestimating incumbents' ability to adapt vs. new entrants' speed advantage
example_applications: Netflix displacing Blockbuster through streaming
Digital photography destroying Kodak's film business
Ride-sharing restructuring urban transportation and taxi licensing markets

topics.label: Human Forces Behind Emerging Technology Creation
objectives: Identify psychological, social, and cultural drivers of technology creation
Explain how individual motivation and team dynamics shape innovation
Analyze the role of government, academia, and industry in technology ecosystems
Evaluate how human biases affect technology design and adoption
common_confusions: Attributing innovation solely to lone genius rather than collaborative ecosystems
Overlooking how cultural and political values shape what gets built and funded
Conflating user needs (stated) with user behaviors (revealed)
example_applications: How DARPA's mission-driven funding model shaped the internet
Silicon Valley's failure-tolerant culture compared to European risk aversion
How algorithmic bias emerges from non-representative development teams

topics.label: Technology Creation
objectives: Describe the stages of the technology creation process from research to prototype
Distinguish between basic research, applied research, and development
Explain the role of intellectual property in protecting and commercializing technology
Analyze how resource allocation decisions shape the direction of new technology
common_confusions: Conflating research (knowledge creation) with development (product realization)
Assuming patents always lead to commercialization — many are defensive or dormant
Underestimating the 'valley of death' between research and market-ready product
example_applications: How mRNA vaccine technology went from academic research to commercial product
SpaceX's approach to compressing the R&D-to-deployment cycle
University technology transfer offices and startup spinouts

topics.label: Creation by Imitation
objectives: Explain the role of imitation and reverse engineering in technology diffusion
Distinguish between fast-follower strategies and pure imitation
Analyze cases where imitation led to superior or differentiated products
Evaluate the ethical and legal boundaries of technology imitation
key_concepts: Learning by doing vs. learning by watching
common_confusions: Assuming first-mover advantage always wins — fast followers often outperform pioneers
Conflating imitation with plagiarism; many imitation strategies are legal and strategic
Overlooking how imitators add value through refinement, localization, or cost reduction
example_applications: Samsung's fast-follower strategy in consumer electronics vs. Apple
Alibaba modeling early features on eBay and Amazon, then localizing for China
Generic pharmaceutical manufacturers using reverse engineering after patent expiry

topics.label: Invention, Innovation and Entrepreneurship
objectives: Distinguish between invention (new idea), innovation (applied idea), and entrepreneurship (commercialization)
Describe the entrepreneurial process from opportunity recognition to scaling
Analyze the role of business models in converting invention into economic value
Evaluate what separates successful from failed technology ventures
common_confusions: Treating invention and innovation as synonymous — innovation requires adoption and value creation
Overemphasizing the idea vs. execution — most successful companies win on execution, not unique ideas
Confusing entrepreneurship with startups — entrepreneurship occurs in large organizations too (intrapreneurship)
example_applications: Xerox PARC invented the GUI; Apple innovated it into a mass-market product
Airbnb's innovation was a business model (asset-light accommodation), not a new technology
Google's PageRank as an academic invention commercialized through advertising innovation

topics.label: Diffusion of Innovation
objectives: Apply Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation framework to a technology example
Identify adopter categories and their behavioral characteristics
Explain the role of social networks and opinion leaders in diffusion
Analyze barriers to adoption and strategies to overcome them
key_concepts: Adopter categories (innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, laggards)
Relative advantage, compatibility, complexity, trialability, observability
common_confusions: Assuming all technologies cross the chasm — many fail between early adopters and mainstream
Treating adopter categories as fixed personality types rather than context-dependent behaviors
Overlooking that different markets have different diffusion curves for the same technology
example_applications: EV adoption crossing the chasm in Norway vs. United States
COVID-19 vaccines showing different adoption curves across demographic groups
Telemedicine's rapid diffusion triggered by the pandemic — a constraint removal case

topics.label: User-Led Adoption of Emerging Technologies
objectives: Explain the concept of lead user innovation and user-driven technology development
Identify conditions under which users become co-innovators
Analyze how user communities shape product evolution
Evaluate the business implications of open vs. closed user innovation ecosystems
common_confusions: Confusing user adoption with user innovation — not all adopters innovate
Assuming companies always benefit from user innovation — sometimes it disrupts their control
Overlooking how platform governance shapes what users are allowed to create or modify
example_applications: Reddit and Wikipedia as user-co-created platforms that outpaced corporate alternatives
3D printing communities developing new filaments and designs beyond manufacturer intent
GitHub enabling developer communities to shape open-source software direction

topics.label: Emerging Technologies Classification and Adoption Strategy
objectives: Apply classification frameworks to categorize emerging technologies by maturity and impact
Develop an adoption strategy for an organization based on technology classification
Evaluate the risk-return profile of early vs. late adoption
Select appropriate assessment tools for tracking technology maturity
common_confusions: Assuming a single adoption strategy fits all technologies across all industries
Conflating technology maturity with organizational readiness — they are separate dimensions
Treating technology classification as static — the same technology may be emerging in one sector and mature in another
example_applications: A healthcare system deciding when to adopt AI diagnostics vs. waiting for FDA validation
McKinsey's 3-Horizons framework applied to a bank's fintech strategy
Gartner's Magic Quadrant as a classification tool for enterprise software selection

topics.label: Implementing Emerging Technologies — Proof of Concept (POC)
objectives: Define the purpose and scope of a Proof of Concept in technology implementation
Design a POC that validates the most critical assumptions of a technology initiative
Identify success criteria and failure modes for a POC
Distinguish a POC from a pilot, MVP, and full deployment
key_concepts: Technology risk reduction through staged implementation
common_confusions: Treating a POC as a miniature production system — scope and quality standards differ intentionally
Confusing POC success with business case validation — technical feasibility ≠ commercial viability
Skipping stakeholder alignment before POC — misaligned expectations cause POC-to-production failures
example_applications: A retailer running a POC to test RFID inventory tracking in one store before chain-wide rollout
A bank validating a blockchain trade finance concept with two counterparties before scaling
An airline testing AI-powered predictive maintenance on one aircraft type before fleet deployment

topics.label: Implementing Emerging Technologies — Proof of Value (POV)
objectives: Define the Proof of Value stage and how it differs from a POC
Design a POV that quantifies business impact and ROI
Identify the organizational and change management requirements for a successful POV
Build a business case for scaling a technology from POV to full deployment
common_confusions: Assuming a successful POC automatically justifies a POV — POC shows it can work, POV shows it should scale
Underestimating organizational resistance as a failure mode for POV — the technology may work, but adoption may not
Conflating POV with full deployment — POV is still a bounded, controlled test environment
example_applications: A hospital system running a POV for AI-assisted radiology across three departments to measure throughput and accuracy ROI
A logistics company quantifying fuel savings from route optimization AI before national rollout
An insurer measuring claims processing speed and fraud detection uplift in a POV before enterprise deployment
